title: Go Zero Values
content:
### Zero Values in Go

In Go, variables declared without an explicit initial value are assigned a **zero value**.

### Zero Values by Type

```go
var i int       // 0
var f float64   // 0.0
var b bool      // false
var s string    // ""
var p *int      // nil
var arr [3]int  // [0, 0, 0]
var sl []int    // nil
var m map[string]int // nil
var ch chan int  // nil
var fn func()    // nil
var iface interface{} // nil
var st struct{}  // {}
```

### Zero Value Table

| Type | Zero Value |
|------|------------|
| All integer types | `0` |
| All float types | `0.0` |
| bool | `false` |
| string | `""` (empty string) |
| pointer | `nil` |
| slice | `nil` |
| map | `nil` |
| channel | `nil` |
| function | `nil` |
| interface | `nil` |
| array | Zero for each element |
| struct | Zero for each field |

### Practical Use

Zero values make Go programs safer - variables are never uninitialized:

```go
var counter int
counter++ // safe, counter starts at 0

var buffer string
buffer += "data" // safe, buffer starts as ""
```

### Checking for Zero Values

```go
if counter == 0 {
    fmt.Println("counter is at zero value")
}

if buffer == "" {
    fmt.Println("buffer is empty")
}
```

> **Key Point:** Unlike C/C++, Go variables are never left uninitialized. This prevents a whole class of bugs caused by undefined behavior.
codeExample:
package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    var i int
    var f float64
    var b bool
    var s string
    var p *int

    fmt.Printf("int:    %d\n", i)
    fmt.Printf("float64: %.1f\n", f)
    fmt.Printf("bool:   %t\n", b)
    fmt.Printf("string: %q\n", s)
    fmt.Printf("pointer: %v\n", p)

    if s == "" {
        fmt.Println("String is zero value (empty)")
    }
}

quiz.question: What is the zero value of a string?
What is the zero value of a pointer?
